revision.text:
[[ja:蟻通神社 (泉佐野市)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Aritōshi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Aritōshi Shrine Worship Hall in Izumisano.jpg|250px|Aritōshi Shrine Worship Hall]]
Aritōshi Shrine Worship Hall (Registered Tangible Cultural Property) | location = 814 Nagataki, Izumisano City, Osaka Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|34|23|8.1|N|135|18|48.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} | Deities = Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Aritōshi Myōjin | type = Former Gō-sha | consecration_year = Traditionally said to be in 93 CE | Main Hall = One-bay Kasuga-zukuri with sugi wood and copper roofing | alternate_name = Aritōshi Myōjin | Main Festival = 2nd Sunday of April (festival) }} '''Aritōshi Shrine''' (蟻通神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Izumisano City]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. The shrine was historically known as Aritōshi Myōjin and holds the status of a former [[Gōsha]] (village shrine). == Deities == * Main deities: [[Ōkuninushi no Mikoto]], Aritōshi Myōjin == History == === Founding=== According to shrine traditions, the shrine was established during the reign of Emperor Kaika, the 9th Emperor of Japan. It is said to have been founded in 93 CE during the mid-{{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} to pray for agricultural prosperity and land development.[https://mai-ru.jp/shrines_and_temples/aritooshijinja/ Aritōshi Shrine] The original location was about 1 km north of its current site along the [[Kumano Kōdō]] (a segment overlapping with the [[Kishū Kaidō]] and modern [[Osaka Prefecture Route 64]]). Historically, the shrine was paired with [[Hine Shrine]] in the upper reaches of the Kashiigawa River, representing the "Sun God" and "Root God," respectively. Together, they formed a significant part of the local spiritual identity.Aritōshi Shrine Historical Documents The name "Aritōshi" first appears in the collection attributed to {{ill|Ki no Tsurayuki|en|Ki no Tsurayuki|qid=Q468288|lt=Ki no Tsurayuki|1=Ki no Tsurayuki}}. Tsurayuki recounted an incident where he inquired about the shrine after a sudden incident involving his horse. Locals referred to the deity as "Aritōhōshi no Kami." Over time, the spelling evolved from "有通神" to "蟻通," influenced by later literature and legends. === Development in Later Periods=== In the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the name "Aritōshi" became widely known due to its inclusion in the noh play "蟻通 (Aritōshi)" by [[:en:Zeami|Zeami]], which was based on a story of divine intervention during Tsurayuki's visit. The tale involves the clever solution of passing thread through a convoluted gem by attaching it to an ant, a task that metaphorically linked to the deity's name. Throughout history, the shrine has faced destruction and rebuilding. For example, during {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}'s [[Kishū Conquest]], the shrine was burned down. It was later rebuilt under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|qid=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}}, who contributed to its reconstruction, including the main hall and associated buildings. In 1942, the shrine was relocated to its current site due to the construction of the Sano Army Airfield, and its buildings were carefully reconstructed. Today, the site is recognized as a National [[Registered Tangible Cultural Property]]. == Cultural Assets == === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties=== The shrine complex includes 11 registered buildings, such as: * Main Hall * Worship Hall and Heiden (Offering Hall) * Noh Stage * Outer Gates and Bridges === Izumisano City Designations=== * Painted ema boards depicting horses and the Thirty-Six Poetry Immortals * A collection of one hundred waka poems dedicated to Aritōshi Myōjin == Festivals == * '''April (2nd Sunday)''': festival (祈年祭) * '''September''': Aritōshi Takigi Noh (薪能), featuring performances of traditional noh, including the play "Aritōshi." * '''October''': Autumn Festival, including a traditional float (danjiri) procession. == Access == * A 15-minute walk from {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|qid=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}}'s {{ill|Hanwa Line|en|Hanwa Line|qid=Q1154800|lt=Hanwa Line|1=Hanwa Line}} {{ill|Nagataki Station|en|Nagataki Station|qid=Q857423|lt=Nagataki Station|1=Nagataki Station}} * Community buses stop near the shrine. == External Links == * [http://www.aritooshi.org/ Aritōshi Shrine Official Website] * [https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/search?freetext=%25E8%259F%25BB%25E9%2580%259A%25E7%25A5%259E%25E7%25A4%25BE Cultural Heritage Online - Aritōshi Shrine] {{Shinto-stub|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|蟻通神社 (泉佐野市)|version=100895373|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:蟻通神社]] [[ja:蟻通神社 (泉佐野市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11625903}} [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Osaka prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Izumisano, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Izumisano, Osaka]]
